title: How a request becomes a result
description: A three-lesson execution-first course built from a pinned fixture repository.

chapters.title: Request flow
chapters.question: How does input become a result?

lessons.title: Why validate before dispatch?
lessons.question: What must happen before a request can be dispatched?

sections.title: The observable problem
sections.whyItMatters: The validation boundary decides whether downstream work is safe.

sections.title: The exact validation code
sections.code:
const text = request.text.trim();
  if (text.length === 0) return { kind: "rejected", value: "empty request" };

sections.title: Mental model
points: Validate at the boundary before dispatching work.

summary: Normalize and validate at the entry boundary.
lessons.mentalModel: The boundary is a filter before the pipeline.

lessons.title: Where does an empty request go?
lessons.question: Which branch handles an empty request?

sections.title: Predict the branch
sections.prompt: What happens after text.length is zero?
sections.explanation: The function returns a rejected result before dispatch.

steps.label: Return rejection
steps.description: Stop with an observable error result.

sections.title: Mental model
points: Error paths are part of the primary execution trace.

summary: The empty-input branch ends the trace with a rejection.
lessons.mentalModel: A branch is a responsibility boundary with distinct outcomes.

lessons.title: How does accepted text become a result?
lessons.question: What does dispatch change before returning?

sections.title: The dispatch implementation
Checkpoint
sections.prompt: Where would you look if accepted text is not uppercased?

options.label: The entry boundary
options.explanation: The boundary trims and validates only.

lessons.mentalModel: Follow data ownership to find the right change point.
